item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This reminded me a lot of the new Wolfensteins, and I love those games and general setting. Upvoted

item.title: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Edit: timer expired, edits made

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

At the end of the SCP, in the letter from the GOC, the '"McCarthy-Truman UFO Scandal" of 1951' is alluded to. This is probably referring to the Roswell UFO Incident. Out of all of the UFO SCPs I know about, I decided that SCP-1051 would be the best fit for this blackbox, as it could easily be an "egg" that got lassoed down by Foundation personnel and spotted. Unless this is referring to something else that I don't know about, in which case, please tell me.

Editing this:

Certainly, an organization whose early history includes a shameful involvement in the "McCarthy-Truman UFO Scandal" of 1951 must understand the value of repentance and restitution.

To this:

Certainly, an organization whose early history includes a shameful involvement in the "McCarthy-Truman UFO Scandal" of 1951 must understand the value of repentance and restitution.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nazi ufo. Really?

Downvoted with force of thousand suns.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It has to do with the origin of the Global Occult Coalition. Specifically, the dead GOC operatives are the FIRST GOC Strike Team. In Global Occult Coalition culture (in-universe), the first Strike Team is basically worshipped; the GOC equivalents of the Guards at the Tomb of the Unknown Soldier literally keep a couple crypts open for them. The Strike Team members were Nazis who denounced their loyalty to the Führer and gave their lives for the betterment of mankind. They just so happened to crash a Nazi flying saucer into the desert just outside of Roswell, New Mexico in the process.

If that doesn't appeal to you, just consider it a tribute to pulp magazines.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know Nazi UFOs were/are a common pulp sci-fi trope, but it seemed way too similar to the first Captain America movie, especially the flight recorder transcript part. Unless I'm missing something (always possible) it doesn't seem to transcend the cliché. The writing is good enough to get a neutral vote though

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvote, but reluctantly so. Started as a typical UFO shtick, then human-made one, plus the whole argument of "give us our shit back, Foundation!" undercurrent, but damn if it didn't treat pulp sci-fi well. No playing with it, no camp beyond what is inherent, just a straight pulp sci-fi SCP that doesn't go into narm land.

In the end, that's what won me over.

item.title: Upvoted harder than an atom bomb.
item.content:encoded:
Crazy laser-wielding power-armor-wearing UFO-piloting Nazis?

Yes. All of the yes. For a thousand years and more, yes.

+1'd

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Glad to see that D. C. al Fine's signature is still being put to good use. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Despite how clunky it can be, I really like this one for it's interesting perspective on the GOC (that hint about the Foundation and McCarthy…) and for the fun pulp of the past. Somewhat enthused upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

I'm not upvoting this in the hope that it wins 2000, but in the sincere hope that this makes it to a mainlister eventually. One thing I would personally like to see is a slightly expanded reason for this:

"Following the accidental deaths of Dr. ██████████ and the remainder of the SCP-2000 research team, no further research into SCP-2000 is to be carried out without a unanimous consent of the O5 council."

And the other overwhelming containment procedures.

The Foundation has far weirder, far more dangerous, far more powerful skips that the Foundation doesn't guard this well, or require unanimous O5 approval just to test, so just what happened to make them guard this thing so much? It's pretty tame stuff as far as the Foundation is concerned. I mean, is there an unanimous urge to try and use the equipment? Are there GOC plants all over the place trying to steal it? And you give us that note, and then not even so much as a hint at it later.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
hurrdurr Roswell. The addendum pretty much confirmed it for me.

Tempted to downvote because all the popular kids are doing it, but this is well enough written for me to vote.

The transcripts were completely unnecessary, though, and sort of killed the suspense for me =/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh my God, that flight log made me laugh. The dialogue is good cheesy sci-fi.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mein Gott…Das ist Eisen Himmel!

Sorry, I'm gonna remain neutral for now, but the similarities got me to laugh, at least.

(this article is especially strange seeing as I ran into a German WW2 vet just the other day-nice guy. He's writing a book)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not a fan of this. I've never developed nostalgia for golden age SF, so the log was very underwhelming. It was obviously deliberately cliched, deliberately in that style, but it didn't come off as fun. Outside of that, it's still underwhelming. A Nazi UFO is an idea that's been done a bunch, and this didn't exactly add any memorable twists to it. I did like the last note, but it wasn't enough to save this from a downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's not much here, but I'm upvoting for goddamn space Nazis. I also love the little jabs in the GOC letter and how the dude's name is so obviously a pseudonywait is that GOC Clef?

(Also, it's "Gesellschaft", one s.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmmmm. This doesn't do much for me until the final note, which I really like. I also like the X-ray laser guns. And the signature image. Both nice touches.

But the rest… Eh. Honestly, I mostly find the GOC underwhelming, but I'm not sure how much of that is a personal reaction to how often I've heard people go on about how the GOC is perfect~ and could kill anything~ and is so much more betterer and more competent and way way way way way!more powerful and cooler than the Foundation is, and they understand all anomalous things despite being militaristic instead of researcher-focused, which is another reason they're superior of course!

… which isn't the fault of this article, but I do wonder if it's altering my perspective in the same way that a lot of people hate hate AWCY and that alters their perspective. While I don't hate any version of the GOC, I haven't been impressed with this version. (Of course, AWCY is also way more over-exposed than the GOC, which is mostly present in casual discussion on IRC, not on the actual site.)

And the final note, despite me enjoying it quite a bit, seemed as aggrandizing of the GOC as per usual. But is that because of my annoyance, or is it because it's actually a problem? At least in the case of the note, I suspect it might be because of my annoyance. Because generally the reason I dislike hearing about the GOC is because it's so generic in its supposed superiority (military is winnar all teh time amirite), but "the occult fallout of the war" isn't really generic. I find it charming.

But I do think that most of the other elements presented here really are bland and generic. I do like the golden-age-sci-fi references in the logs, but the logs are still plain ole cliche. And the fact is, too, that I read through half the article before knowing it was the GOC and I was only interested by the X-ray lasers.

Also, bonus points for at least one female Cool GOC Agent.

Overall… not sure how to vote at the moment. Hopefully this comment is helpful; I'm unsure of that as well, to be honest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this now that you rewrote parts of it.

What is the McCarthy-Truman UFO scandal? SCP-1051?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed, it was petty. Removing the note.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I, sincerely, love this. Love this too much…

Also:

SCP-2000-C1 through C10: The cremated remains of twelve individuals

C1 to C10 = ten individuals, yet the text says that there are twelve individuals… ·<·

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconding this. The expunged containment procedures contributed to my original downvote, but the note seems like the researcher who wrote it is making a poorly thought out jab at those who requested the containment procedures to be unexpunged. My downvote stays.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really don't like that note. It comes across as childish and petty, and not what you'd expect of a doctor senior enough to put his own notes on containment procedures.

In fact, I disliked it enough that I changed my upvote to a downvote, even though I liked the post-pulp setting hugely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted with glee. I'm a sucker for stuff like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good idea. I'll replace it with the "sigrunen." My main reason for using Werwolf instead was the alleged order that members of that unit were ordered to resist to the last even after the war had ended.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not bad, honestly. One large cavil, though:

The Werwolf group was a commando group trained for operation behind enemy lines. Here's their flag:

No unspoilered images in the forums.
Sincerely, Scantron.

That design, if you're curious, is a Wolfsangel, a heraldic device representing a "wolf-hook". You attached a chain to a tree, a Wolfsangel to the chain, and a bait to the Wolfsangel. The wolf takes the bait and, well, you can guess what happens next. It, like the name for the organization, was given to them from the 1910 book Der Wehrwolf, about a man named Harm Wulf who organizes resistance during the Thirty Years' War.

Yes, the Nazis stole ideas from novels. Moving on.

You've got the Deutsches Ahnenerbe (literally "German Inheritance of the forefathers", more commonly "German ancestral heritage"), so it might be best to replace the Werwolf members with the Schutzstaffel, given that Ahnenerbe was founded by Himmler, the man who gave the SS their place in history. This would be in keeping with the Ahnenerbe using the SS during their theft of the Veit Stoss altar from Krakow, Poland, the attempted theft of the Codex Aesinas from Ancona, and the attempted removal of the Bayeux Tapestry from the Louvre. (These are the same people who inspired the villains in Raiders of the Lost Ark, you know.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Solves my problem.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well at least there are containment procedures now. The note was somewhat childish but hey whatever floats your boat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fine. Are you happy now?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ah, thanks. I may re-edit my post after the re-read.

EDIT: Well, that is better, thanks. You might want to make a specification or two regarding how far away personnel should stand, though — if they don't know what the enti-theft measures are, they need to at least know how not to set them off.

I'll comment on the article itself separately.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why has this become such a set-in-stone rule? There used to be tons of articles, tons of good articles, that had expungement in the containment procedures. And it makes sense, too! If the Foundation, as a last resort, will nuke the facility when a containment breach happens, does it want the security guards to know this? No, of course it wouldn't. So if you were to release the document to the security guards, would the last line, "oh btw we nuke this thing and this will absolutely kill you," will that be expunged? Yes!

Er, no. Every facility has a nuclear self-destruct "just in case", and that's well-known to everyone except (possibly) the D-class personnel. When you're containing stuff that makes Ebola look like an upset stomach, understanding just how dead you'll be if you mess up is an incentive not to mess up.

In this case, the defensive measures have been expunged from a document that's supposed to be telling people what to do in case of emergency. That makes about as much sense as a plan "in case of fire" that omits the locations of the fire extinguishers and the existence of the automatic sprinklers. If for no other reason than to tell people to stay out of their way, the defensive measures need to be identified in the containment procedures.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What containment procedures? The containment procedures are, "Keep them locked away in boxes." What's expunged are the anti-theft measures that would, of course, be classified so as to not give a thief a road map of how to steal these things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Re: Expunged containment procedures

I think people misunderstand what's being expunged. I've edited the article to clarify it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For some people, the containment procedures are the in-universe justification for the document's existence. The Foundation doesn't write up objects that it contains just because they're interesting. Sharing is not caring, everything is need-to-know and the only thing anyone needs to know is how to keep skips from eating people, or worse, blowing the Masquerade.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree with your premise but your conclusion seems improbable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The whole in universe point of containment procedures is that the people working with the skip know how to contain it. It kills my suspension of disbelief that the people who assigned to contain a skip are not allowed to read on how to contain it. It is literally the only part of the article that can't afford to be expunged.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, I'd really like to talk about expunging things in the containment procedures.

Why has this become such a set-in-stone rule? There used to be tons of articles, tons of good articles, that had expungement in the containment procedures. And it makes sense, too! If the Foundation, as a last resort, will nuke the facility when a containment breach happens, does it want the security guards to know this? No, of course it wouldn't. So if you were to release the document to the security guards, would the last line, "oh btw we nuke this thing and this will absolutely kill you," will that be expunged? Yes!

If part of the Foundation's procedures for containing an object involves laser guns and remote-controlled nanotech grey goo that will deploy in the event of containment breach, does it want people to know that it has tech this advanced? Does it want to just plaster this information on every single document related to the SCP that it gives out? No, of course it wouldn't!

There isn't a list of stupid rules, that you go down and if you break one game over time to press the -1 button. I'm getting the impression that people are given advice which is phrased like a rule when in actuality, like, there is really one rule and if you break that rule press the -1 article: "Don't write a bad article."

Also, the dialogue sold it for me. Go big or go home, or in this case, load up on as much ham and cheese as you can, or go home. Plus I liked the last note. Like, I said the story was too subtle. Got my story, and it's still subtle. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Downvoted for expungment of containment procedures regardless of the quality of the rest of the article. I guess the author believes his seniority allows for such a fundamental part of the process to be left out. Can't say I agree.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I get what you're trying to do here. The problem is that directly porting over pulp sci-fi into an SCP article, with little in the way of translation apart from the format and specific GOI references, retains the problems of that medium. And by "problems", I mean the played-straight Space Nazis, the powered armor suits that for some reason have to actually draw their weapons out of literal holsters, and the awful dialogue in the second-to-last addendum that is supposed to simultaneously be all like "that '50s sci-fi, huh guys? Huh?" while setting us up for the last, overly serious addendum.

I'm not against Space Nazis packing x-ray weaponry on general principle, though it's been a little too overdone in the recent past IMO. But it needs a lot more finesse than what this article has to offer. -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very authentic. +1

(Also, why did the article got many downvotes? It's actually not that bad.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm assuming that the expungment/absence of containment procedures is intentional sandbagging, but either way, -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There is a unneeded "F" under the SCP-2000-A paragraph.

At first, I was interested why there is no research taking place, but that was never answered, sadly. It probably would be in the expungements, but they give little to think about.

We have a possibly… Nazi spaceship, invaded by the GOCs, or vice versa, and some well thought hi-tech hardware.

I'm not saying this is bad, but it's not a good candidate for the 2000 spot. I'd really appreciate some more hinting on what is going on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was really curious as to what kind of takes on old pulp sci-fi would pop up during the contest…but I gotta say, I'm disappointed. Far too straightforward for my tastes.

Edit: The new additions are entertaining, but I still can't quite get behind what this article is putting out there. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Classic Dr. Clef. It's not high caliber material and there are flaws here and there that remind me of the old school SCPs. However, it's just entertaining enough to get my attention, even if it has the Stupid Jetpack Hitler trope.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not feeling it. And I have had it repeatedly drilled into my head that you do not expunge containment procedures. I'll be generous and vote neutral because of some earlier comments giving insight into what I couldn't figure out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm upvoting for the concept alone, but I will agree that this feels… stilted. I think a rewrite is in order.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The "Good Guy GOC" hook and the fact that it's thoroughly golden-age sci-fi balances out that I feel like this could've been presented in a number of other ways that would've sold me on this. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this concept could work better as a tale. The SCP format isn't doing it too many favors, I don't think.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Point it may be, I still do not find it a particularly interesting or effective point. It's like the expungement, in a way. There could be something really cool here, but there isn't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The fun stuff has already happened and we get nothing but the echoes.

That's the point. This is what happens after the pulp fiction book is over. After "happily ever after", so to speak.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well written and entertaining. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm. It's old school science fiction, and the article's aesthetic kind of reminds me of what something would look like on this site three years ago. I can't really complain about that. The part about the GOC has potential, but all the same, that potential isn't realized. The story is almost too subtle in this article. I don't want to say that you should pull that thing where the article is just an excuse for a tale, but maybe a bit more on the operatives? I'm voting neutral.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Defensive measures in place are to include [RUNNING AROUND IN CIRCLES AND SCREAMING]

Autopsies carried out after the initial crash site recovery indicates that all ten individuals were killed [BY WALRUS FARTS.]

SCP-2000-F through Z: [DATA EXPUNGED UNDER SECURITY PROTOCOL 999-2000-GERNSBACK]

SCP-2000-ALEPH through HE: [DATA EXPUNGED UNDER SECURITY PROTOCOL 999-2000-GERNSBACK]

This just comes across as excessive, and I am not interested in what is behind them, because I do not believe that anything is actually behind them. Instead of being interesting, it's simply irritating.

Beyond that…it's Space Nazis, and it's not nearly as exciting or interesting as it could or should be. The fun stuff has already happened and we get nothing but the echoes.

-1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is just Stupid Jetpack Hitler Nazitech without any real twist on the concept. You try to disguise it as an alien crash, when really, it would work better the other way around! Why not have the cover story be Nazitech, but have the real twist be aliens? Also, you expunged the Containment procedures. What the actual fuck.

-1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And VAV3 was the plucky younger sidekick. VAV4 was the good-hearted rival agent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
PROTOCOL 999-2000-GERNSBACK

I see what you did there

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like that this is based on & references old-school scifi. The genre itself seems almost cute to us now, because the science was wrong based on later understanding, but it would have been deadly serious if it had been real.

Also, I'm assuming that -VAV1 & -VAV2 are the chiseled-jaw hero and his feminine-but-competent-for-a-woman love interest?
